content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
Technical Assistance • • • Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660          322-5901          322-6441          322-6444

May 4, 1979

Kirk Pessner
Dobbs and Nielsen
Suite 2500, The Alcoa Building
One Maritime Plaza
San Francisco, CA 94111

Dear Kirk:

     This will confirm today's telephone conversation when
we discussed the reporting requirements concerning ticket
sales for campaign-sponsored concerts or other events handled
by Ticketron.

     I advised you that Ticketron or another commercial firm
handling ticket sales is not required to obtain the name,
address, occupation and employer, or place of business if
self-employed, of each person who buys a ticket assuming the
price of one ticket to be less than $100. However, Ticketron
will have to obtain that identifying information if a person
buys tickets totaling $100 or more at one time.

     I did find an advice memorandum containing the reporting
standards for concert funds. Of course, the $50 threshold
referred to in the memorandum is now $100. I am enclosing a
copy of that memorandum for your information.

     If you have any questions, please feel free to call.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Barbara
                                        Barbara Campbell
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

BC:plh
Enclosure

84210(g)

Concert Funds - Reporting Standards

At the opinion request meeting of June 24, 1977,

the staff considered the question of how the proceeds of a

concert should be reported pursuant to Section 84210(g). The

entertainer donated his time as volunteer personal services

and the tickets which cost $3 each were sold through Ticketron.

The filer should request Ticketron to keep records

of any person which they know purchased tickets valued at

$50 or more. However, if Ticketron refuses, concert tickets

still may be sold through that agency. If through the use

of reasonable diligence, the filer becomes aware of any per-

son who has purchased tickets of $50 or more in value, he

must report that person as an itemized contributor on his

campaign statement.

All of the rest of the proceeds must be reported

as contributions of less than $50 in value. If the filer

wishes, he may attach a supplement to his campaign statement

stating the time and place of the concert, the name of the

performer and the amount of money raised. Filing in accordance

with this procedure would further the purposes of disclosure

and simplify the auditor's task.

125

sections.question_synthetic: What are the reporting requirements for ticket sales from campaign-sponsored concerts or events handled by commercial ticket vendors like Ticketron, specifically regarding when identifying information must be collected?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Commercial ticket vendors like Ticketron are not required to collect identifying information for ticket purchases under $100, but must collect such information for purchases of $100 or more in a single transaction. The filer should request the vendor keep records of purchases of $100 or more (updated from the previous $50 threshold), and must report those as itemized contributions while reporting smaller amounts as unitemized contributions.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: No formal QUESTION and CONCLUSION sections present; letter is in conversational format confirming telephone discussion. Includes attached memorandum on concert fund reporting standards from June 1977, which established the original $50 threshold now updated to $100. Counsel is Barbara Campbell.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What are the reporting requirements for ticket sales from campaign-sponsored concerts or events handled by commercial ticket vendors like Ticketron, specifically regarding when identifying information must be collected?

CONCLUSION: Commercial ticket vendors like Ticketron are not required to collect identifying information for ticket purchases under $100, but must collect such information for purchases of $100 or more in a single transaction. The filer should request the vendor keep records of purchases of $100 or more (updated from the previous $50 threshold), and must report those as itemized contributions while reporting smaller amounts as unitemized contributions.

I advised you that Ticketron or another commercial firm handling ticket sales is not required to obtain the name, address, occupation and employer, or place of business if self-employed, of each person who buys a ticket assuming the price of one ticket to be less than $100. However, Ticketron will have to obtain that identifying information if a person buys tickets totaling $100 or more at one time. I did find an advice memorandum containing the reporting standards for concert funds. Of course, the $50 threshold referred to in the memorandum is now $100. I am enclosing a copy of that memorandum for your information. If you have any questions, please feel free to call. Sincerely, Barbara Barbara Campbell Counsel Legal Division BC:plh Enclosure 84210(g) Concert Funds - Reporting Standards At the opinion request meeting of June 24, 1977, the staff considered the question of how the proceeds of a concert should be reported pursuant to Section 84210(g). The entertainer donated his time as volunteer personal services and the tickets which cost $3 each were sold through Ticketron. The filer should request Ticketron to keep records of any person which they know purchased tickets valued at $50 or more. However, if Ticketron refuses, concert tickets still may be sold through that agency. If through the use of reasonable diligence, the filer becomes aware of any per- son who has purchased tickets of $50 or more in value, he must report that person as an itemized contributor on his campaign statement. All of the rest of the proceeds must be reported as contributions of less than $50 in value. If the filer wishes, he may attach a supplement to his campaign statement stating the time and place of the concert, the name of the performer and the amount of money raised. Filing in accordance with this procedure would further the purposes of disclosure and simplify the auditor's task. 125
embedding.summary: FPPC counsel advises on reporting requirements for campaign-sponsored event ticket sales through commercial vendors like Ticketron, clarifying that identifying information collection thresholds are $100 (updated from $50) and providing standards for itemized versus unitemized contribution reporting.
